summary.difficultyAssessment: Distribution is D1:82 (36%), D2:86 (38%), D3:57 (25%). D1 is slightly overweight vs GL target (~30%) and D3 is underweight vs target (~30%). Many D1 questions are near-identical in structure (e.g. straight line: 180 - known = answer, repeated ~20 times with only the number changing). The algebraic angle questions at D2 suffer from severe repetition — 15+ questions all follow the identical pattern 'coefficients sum to 18, answer is always 10' (Q71, Q79, Q84, Q89, Q91, Q94, Q98, Q103, Q107, Q110, Q112, Q116). D3 questions on parallel lines are also repetitive — all ask 'what is the corresponding/alternate angle?' with the answer always being the given angle itself.
summary.coverageAssessment: Good coverage of core angle rules: straight line (180), around a point (360), triangle angles, quadrilateral angles, isosceles, right-angled triangles, algebraic angles, exterior angles, parallel lines, and polygon interior angles. Coverage gaps: (1) Vertically opposite angles — only 2 questions (Q133, Q134) use this concept and none are standalone questions testing it as a primary skill. (2) Co-interior angles with parallel lines — only Q188, Q220, Q221 test this (3 of 225). (3) No questions test angle estimation/measurement reading skills. (4) No questions on symmetry and angle relationships. (5) No 'which of these shapes has X property' classification questions. (6) Missing angle sums for irregular polygons beyond hexagon/heptagon are over-represented at D3 relative to GL weighting.
summary.teachingEfficacyAssessment: 22 lessons across 11 sub-concepts provide strong structural coverage. Each sub-concept has a primary lesson (step-by-step or visual-discovery) plus a secondary lesson (spot-the-mistake or curiosity-hook), which is excellent pedagogy. Visual components (AngleDisplay, AngleDiagram, QuadShape, ParallelLines, ExteriorAngle, RegularPolygon, WorkedExample) are well-used. However, the mapping data shows '[No lesson found]' for ALL question-lesson teach summaries, suggesting the teach screen body text is null/missing in the extraction — this needs verification. The master-missing-angle-triangle sub-concept has no learning goals in the mapping (Q3, Q5, Q24, Q25 show empty lessonLearningGoal arrays), suggesting incomplete lesson configuration. Lessons appropriately address common errors (confusing 180/360, forgetting to divide by 2 in isosceles). The spot-the-mistake template is particularly effective for this topic.
topPriorities: Fix the algebraic angle questions where 15+ D2 questions all have the identical answer of 10 — these provide zero additional learning value and should be diversified
Increase D3 count from 57 to ~65-68 and reduce D1 from 82 to ~68-70 to better match GL 30:40:30 distribution
Add vertically opposite angles as a standalone question category with 5-8 dedicated questions
Fix Q7 classification ambiguity — the question asks for triangle type but two valid answers exist (right-angled AND isosceles)
Diversify parallel lines questions beyond 'corresponding/alternate angles are equal' to include co-interior angles, finding unknown angles using multiple rules, and multi-step problems

issues.title: 12 algebraic straight-line questions all produce x=10 via coefficients summing to 18
issues.description: Twelve D2 algebraic angle questions on straight lines all use coefficient pairs that sum to 18 (e.g. 7u+8u+3u, 6y+7y+5y, 8v+10v, 11c+7c, 13g+5g, 9j+6j+3j, 14n+4n, 15r+3r, 10t+5t+3t, 16u+2u, 11w+4w+3w, 17z+z). Every single one gives x=10. A child quickly spots this pattern and can answer all 12 without doing any maths. This completely undermines the learning value. Additionally, Q51 and Q12 both use coefficients summing to 5 (giving x=36) as a pair, making 14 questions with only 2 distinct answers between them.
issues.suggestedFix: Reduce to 4-5 algebraic straight-line questions with genuinely different coefficient sums (e.g. sums of 5, 6, 9, 10, 12, 15 giving answers of 36, 30, 20, 18, 15, 12). Replace the removed questions with more varied algebraic angle problems: angles around a point with algebra, quadrilateral angles with algebra, or multi-step problems combining rules.

issues.title: 10 parallel line questions all have identical structure — answer always equals the given angle
issues.description: All 10 corresponding/alternate angle questions (Q14, Q19, Q35, Q42, Q57, Q68, Q80, Q93, Q106, Q119) follow the exact same pattern: 'One angle is X. What is the corresponding/alternate angle?' The answer is always X. There is zero cognitive variation. A child learns after the first question that the answer is always the same number repeated. These are all rated D3 but offer no genuine D3 challenge since the pattern is trivially learnable.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep 3-4 of these as introductory D1/D2 questions. Replace the rest with genuine D3 parallel line questions: (1) 'Find the co-interior angle' (answer = 180 - given), (2) 'Given one angle, find a non-adjacent angle using two rules', (3) multi-step problems where you must use alternate + straight line rules together. This would also boost the genuinely thin co-interior angle coverage.

issues.title: Corresponding/alternate angle questions mislabelled as D3 when they are D1 in practice
issues.description: Questions like Q14, Q19, Q35, Q42, Q57, Q68, Q80, Q93, Q106, Q119 are all labelled D3. However, the skill tested is simply 'corresponding/alternate angles are equal' — a single-fact recall that requires no calculation, no multi-step reasoning, and no inference. By GL difficulty criteria, single-fact recall is D1. These artificially inflate the D3 count (10 of 57 D3 questions = 18% of all D3). If reclassified correctly, true D3 drops to 47 (21%), making the D3 shortage even more acute.
issues.suggestedFix: Reclassify the simple 'angles are equal' parallel line questions as D1. Create genuine D3 parallel line questions requiring multi-step reasoning (e.g. combining co-interior + vertically opposite, or finding an angle using two parallel line rules in sequence).

issues.title: Regular polygon interior angles and basic parallel line facts labelled D1 but require formula knowledge
issues.description: Q199 (regular hexagon interior angle = 120) and Q200 (regular pentagon = 108) are labelled D1. However, calculating these requires knowing and applying the (n-2) x 180 / n formula, which is Year 6+ content. For a 9-year-old, this is genuinely D2 at minimum. Conversely, Q217-Q219 are basic parallel line questions labelled D1, which is correct — but inconsistent with the D3 labelling of Q14, Q19 etc. which test the identical skill.
issues.suggestedFix: Reclassify Q199 and Q200 as D2. Ensure all 'corresponding/alternate angles are equal' questions share the same difficulty level (D1 for basic recall, D2 if combined with another step).

issues.title: Vertically opposite angles barely covered — only 2 questions, no standalone sub-concept
issues.description: Vertically opposite angles are a KS2 statutory requirement and appear on GL papers. Only Q133 and Q134 touch on this concept, and Q141/Q144 use it as a secondary step. There is no sub-concept, no lesson, and no dedicated question set for this fundamental angle fact. The mapping data has no 'vertically-opposite' sub-concept.
issues.suggestedFix: Create a 'vertically-opposite-angles' sub-concept with 2 lessons and 6-8 dedicated questions: 3 at D1 ('Two lines cross, one angle is 48, what is the opposite angle?'), 3 at D2 (find an angle using vertically opposite + straight line combined), 2 at D3 (multi-step with parallel lines).

issues.title: Co-interior angles (allied angles) severely under-represented — only 3 questions
issues.description: Co-interior angles (same-side angles between parallel lines summing to 180) appear in only Q188, Q220, and Q221. This is a distinct GL-tested concept that children often confuse with alternate/corresponding angles. The current 3 questions are insufficient for practice.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 4-5 co-interior angle questions: 2 at D2 (basic 180 - given), 3 at D3 (combined with other angle rules, or finding both angles in a trapezium using co-interior property).

issues.title: Q7 triangle classification question has two valid answers
issues.description: Q7 asks 'Two angles in a triangle are 45 and 90. What type of triangle is this?' with options Equilateral, Isosceles, Right-angled, Obtuse, Scalene. The correct answer is given as 'Right-angled', but the triangle is also isosceles (two 45 angles). The explanation acknowledges this: 'making it a right-angled isosceles triangle, but right-angled is the main classification'. While the intended answer is defensible, a child who correctly identifies it as isosceles would be marked wrong. GL avoids this ambiguity.
issues.suggestedFix: Either (1) change the angles so the triangle is right-angled but NOT isosceles (e.g. 90 and 30, giving 60), or (2) remove 'Isosceles' from the options so there is no ambiguity.

issues.title: Q45 requires rounding to 1 decimal place — non-standard for GL 11+ maths
issues.description: Q45 asks for the interior angle of a regular heptagon, yielding 128.571... which is rounded to 128.6. GL 11+ maths questions produce whole-number answers. Requiring decimal rounding introduces a skill (rounding) that is extraneous to the geometry being tested and creates a source of error unrelated to angle knowledge.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with a polygon that produces a whole-number answer (pentagon=108, hexagon=120, octagon=135, nonagon=140, decagon=144, dodecagon=150). Or reframe to ask for the total interior angle sum (which is always whole): 'What is the sum of interior angles in a heptagon?' Answer: 900.

issues.title: Q83 tests a 15-sided polygon (pentadecagon) — well beyond GL 11+ scope
issues.description: Q83 asks for the interior angle of a regular 15-sided polygon (pentadecagon). Similarly Q104 asks about a 20-sided polygon (icosagon). GL 11+ maths stays within shapes children encounter in the KS2 curriculum: triangle, quadrilateral, pentagon, hexagon, and occasionally octagon. A 15-sided or 20-sided polygon is not part of the Year 5-6 curriculum and the name 'pentadecagon' would be unfamiliar to virtually all 10-year-olds.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace Q83 and Q104 with questions about more common polygons or with questions that test the formula in a different way (e.g. 'A regular polygon has interior angles of 144 each. How many sides does it have?').

issues.title: Q189 is labelled D3 but is identical in structure to D1 quadrilateral questions
issues.description: Q189 asks 'Three angles of a quadrilateral are 80, 100, and 95. What is the fourth angle?' This is the exact same structure as Q10 (D1), Q26 (D1), Q38 (D1), Q67 (D1), Q86 (D1) — subtract three angles from 360. There is no additional complexity, multi-step reasoning, or inference to justify D3.
issues.suggestedFix: Reclassify Q189 as D1. Ensure consistent difficulty labelling: basic 'subtract from total' questions should all be D1 regardless of which question number they are.

issues.title: 10 basic straight-line questions (D1) are near-identical — simple 180 minus one angle
issues.description: Questions Q1, Q23, Q123, Q124, Q125, Q126, Q127, Q128, Q129, Q130, Q223, Q224 all ask the same thing: two angles on a straight line, one is given, find the other. That is 12 questions testing a single-step subtraction from 180. While some repetition aids fluency, 12 is excessive for a single pattern. Combined with Q131/Q132 (three angles on a straight line, still D1-level), the straight-line sub-concept has ~25 questions at the most basic level.
issues.suggestedFix: Reduce basic two-angle straight-line D1 questions to 5-6. Use the freed slots for more varied angle problems (vertically opposite, multi-rule combinations, real-world contexts).

issues.title: Q210 and Q117 are identical questions — isosceles with two 70-degree angles
issues.description: Q117 (D2): 'An isosceles triangle has two equal angles of 70 each. What is the third angle?' Answer: 40. Q210 (D1): 'An isosceles triangle has two equal angles of 70. What is the third angle?' Answer: 40. These are the exact same question with different difficulty labels.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove one of the duplicates. If keeping Q210 at D1, remove Q117 (or vice versa). Ensure the remaining question has the correct difficulty label — this is a single-step subtraction, so D1 is appropriate.

issues.title: master-missing-angle-triangle sub-concept has no learning goals and no lesson content
issues.description: Questions Q3, Q5, Q24, Q25, Q85, Q96, Q113, Q159-Q163 map to sub-concept 'master-missing-angle-triangle' which shows empty lessonLearningGoal arrays and '[No lesson found]' for teach summaries. This sub-concept appears to be a catch-all for basic triangle angle questions that don't fit isosceles, right-angled, or algebraic categories. These children get no targeted lesson before attempting these questions.
issues.suggestedFix: Either (1) create a proper 'Missing Angle in a Triangle' sub-concept with 2 lessons covering the 180 rule for general triangles, or (2) merge these questions into an existing sub-concept like straight-line (since the method is identical: subtract known from total).

issues.title: All lesson teach summaries show '[No lesson found]' in mappings — possible extraction bug
issues.description: Every single mapping entry shows lessonTeachSummary as '[No lesson found]', even for sub-concepts that clearly have lessons (e.g. straight-line has 2 lessons with full teach screens). This is likely an extraction/wiring issue in the mapping generation script rather than actually missing lessons. However, it means the mapping data cannot confirm whether lessons properly address their mapped questions.
issues.suggestedFix: Investigate the mapping extraction script to determine why lessonTeachSummary is not being populated. The lesson data file confirms teach screens exist with interaction content, so this appears to be a data pipeline issue.

issues.title: Q137 is identical to Q17 — both ask 'one angle is twice the other on a straight line'
issues.description: Q17 (D2): 'Sarah draws two angles on a straight line. One angle is twice the size of the other. What is the smaller angle?' Answer: 60. Q137 (D2): 'Jack draws two angles on a straight line. One angle is twice the size of the other. What is the smaller angle?' Answer: 60. Only the character name differs.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove Q137 or change it to a different ratio (e.g. one angle is 4 times the other: x + 4x = 180, x = 36).

issues.title: Questions 123-225 lack character names and real-world context compared to Q1-Q122
issues.description: The first 122 questions consistently use character names (Tom, Sophie, Emma, Jake, etc.) and sometimes real-world scenarios. Questions 123-225 are more bare and abstract: 'Two angles on a straight line are X and Y. What is Y?' While not incorrect, the shift in style is noticeable. GL papers typically embed maths in light contexts. The warmth and engagement drops noticeably in the second half.
issues.suggestedFix: Add brief character names and contexts to the more abstract questions in the 123-225 range. E.g. 'Sophie measures two angles on a straight line. One is 130. What is the other?' This maintains engagement consistency.

issues.title: Algebraic angle question distractors follow a predictable +/-5 pattern
issues.description: Nearly all D2 algebraic angle questions use distractors spaced exactly 5 apart (e.g. 8, 10, 12, 15, 18 or similar patterns). While evenly-spaced distractors are standard, the consistency of the 5-apart pattern means a child familiar with the format can sometimes eliminate options by recognising the spacing pattern rather than doing the maths.
issues.suggestedFix: Vary distractor spacing: include some with uneven spacing, include common wrong answers (e.g. for x + 2x = 180, include 90 as a distractor since children might divide 180 by 2 instead of 3). Make distractors diagnostic of specific errors.

issues.title: Q190 states kites have 'two pairs of equal adjacent angles' — this is not always how kites are defined at KS2
issues.description: Q190 states 'A kite has two pairs of equal adjacent angles.' At KS2 level, a kite is typically defined as having 2 pairs of equal adjacent sides and exactly 1 pair of equal opposite angles (the angles between the unequal sides). The question's phrasing could confuse children who have learned a different definition. The maths is correct (110 + 110 + x + x = 360, x = 70), but the framing may not match what children have been taught.
issues.suggestedFix: Rephrase to avoid the definitional claim: 'A kite has angles of 110, 110, x, and x. What is x?' or 'A kite has two angles of 110 each. The other two angles are equal. What is each one?'
